critters.name: White-tailed Deer
critters.summary: Midsummer edge grazer now feeding heavy while fawns stay tucked and bucks carry growing velvet antlers.
critters.fact: By early July, white-tailed deer are deep into summer feeding patterns, with does raising fawns and bucks growing velvet antlers.
critters.activeFact: Right now, deer may hit clover, openings, trail edges, and shady browse pockets, while fawns often stay hidden nearby.

critters.name: Black Bear
critters.summary: Summer calorie machine roaming green woods for berries, insects, plants, and every easy snack the forest offers.
critters.fact: In early July, black bears feed heavily on berries, insects, soft plants, carrion, and opportunistic foods to build summer weight.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bears may work berry patches, wet bottoms, logs, trails, and quiet feeding pockets where food is stacking up.

critters.name: Coyote
critters.summary: Lean summer hunter working trails and field edges while pups grow and adults keep the food runs moving.
critters.fact: By early July, coyote pups are growing fast, and adults hunt steadily for rodents, rabbits, insects, birds, and easy meals.
critters.activeFact: Right now, coyotes may travel two-tracks, openings, and brush lines at dawn or dusk while feeding hungry pups.

critters.name: Red Fox
critters.summary: Rusty grassline hunter now chasing rodents and summer insects while young foxes learn the neighborhood.
critters.fact: In early July, red fox kits are more mobile and adults continue hunting rodents, rabbits, birds, insects, and seasonal fruit.
critters.activeFact: Right now, foxes may appear near open grass, trails, and field edges where mice and young prey are active.

critters.name: Gray Fox
critters.summary: Brush-country shadow staying tucked in thick summer cover while quietly feeding young and avoiding open drama.
critters.fact: Gray foxes rely on dense summer vegetation for cover while raising young and hunting small mammals, birds, insects, and fruit.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gray fox activity is most likely along brushy lanes, wooded edges, and shadowed routes where cover is thick.

critters.name: Bobcat
critters.summary: Silent midsummer stalker using heavy green cover to ambush rabbits, rodents, birds, and careless young prey.
critters.fact: By early July, bobcats take advantage of thick vegetation and busy prey movement to hunt along edges and timber cover.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bobcats may work brush lines, crossings, and shaded transition zones where small animals move through cover.

critters.name: Porcupine
critters.summary: Slow leafy browser now enjoying summer greens while still chewing woody snacks like an old habit.
critters.fact: In summer, porcupines feed on leaves, herbs, bark, twigs, and aquatic plants when available.
critters.activeFact: Right now, porcupines may be found in leafy trees, quiet woods, and green edges where soft vegetation is easy to reach.

critters.name: Beaver
critters.summary: Wetland contractor still running night repairs, food cutting, and water-level management like a swamp foreman.
critters.fact: Beavers stay active all summer cutting vegetation, maintaining dams, repairing lodges, and managing pond depth.
critters.activeFact: Right now, fresh mud, peeled sticks, slides, and new cuttings can show where the beaver night crew clocked in.

critters.name: Muskrat
critters.summary: Warm-season pond commuter cruising cattails and green shorelines like a tiny wetland taxi.
critters.fact: Muskrats feed heavily on aquatic plants in summer and use bank burrows, lodges, and shoreline trails.
critters.activeFact: Right now, muskrats may swim pond edges, feed near cattails, or slip through green water lanes at dawn and dusk.

critters.name: Mink
critters.summary: Slick shoreline predator patrolling warm banks where frogs, fish, eggs, and small prey are having a rough July.
critters.fact: Minks hunt fish, frogs, crayfish, eggs, birds, and small mammals along ponds, creeks, and marsh edges.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mink may show along muddy banks, pond margins, and tight shoreline routes where prey is concentrated.

critters.name: Fisher
critters.summary: Deep-woods prowler moving through shaded summer timber where squirrels and rabbits keep making noise.
critters.fact: Fishers use large forest ranges and hunt squirrels, rabbits, birds, and other prey through the warm season.
critters.activeFact: Right now, fishers are mostly unseen in heavy woods, with trail cameras often catching the best proof they passed through.

critters.name: River Otter
critters.summary: Midsummer water clown hunting fish and frogs while turning muddy banks into personal playground equipment.
critters.fact: River otters feed on fish, frogs, crayfish, and aquatic prey while using slides, dens, and waterways for travel.
critters.activeFact: Right now, otters may cruise channels, stir pond edges, use muddy slides, and hunt where warm-water prey is active.

critters.name: Eastern Gray Squirrel
critters.summary: Leafy treetop circus now loaded with young squirrels, feeder raids, shade breaks, and branch chaos.
critters.fact: By early July, young gray squirrels may be active, while adults feed on seeds, fungi, buds, fruit, and garden scraps.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gray squirrels may be busy in full leaf cover, raiding feeders, chasing, and teaching young squirrels the art of nonsense.

critters.name: American Red Squirrel
critters.summary: Tiny red security alarm still defending cones, feeders, and personal space with outrageous confidence.
critters.fact: Red squirrels defend territories year-round and use summer to feed, cache food, and guard prime conifer habitat.
critters.activeFact: Right now, red squirrels may chatter from branches, guard feeding spots, and make the woods sound personally offended.

critters.name: Chipmunk
critters.summary: Striped pocket rocket running full summer traffic between logs, burrows, seeds, and cheek-stuffed errands.
critters.fact: Chipmunks gather seeds, berries, fungi, insects, and other foods in summer, often carrying supplies in cheek pouches.
critters.activeFact: Right now, chipmunks may zip across trails, logs, and rocks before diving into burrow entrances with grocery cheeks.

critters.name: Snowshoe Hare
critters.summary: Brown summer hare disappearing into leafy understory where thick cover finally pays rent.
critters.fact: By July, snowshoe hares wear brown summer coats and rely on dense vegetation for food and camouflage.
critters.activeFact: Right now, hares may be hard to spot in shaded brush, young forest growth, and thick understory where they blend fast.

critters.name: Eastern Cottontail
critters.summary: Midsummer brush-edge hopper grazing clover, lawns, and soft greens with one ear on emergency mode.
critters.fact: Eastern cottontails breed through the warm season and feed heavily on grasses, clover, garden plants, and tender shoots.
critters.activeFact: Right now, cottontails may feed near lawns, trails, clover patches, and brush piles during cooler dawn and dusk windows.

critters.name: Raccoon
critters.summary: Night-shift snack criminal working water edges, feeders, fruit, bugs, eggs, and anything not nailed down.
critters.fact: Raccoons eat crayfish, frogs, insects, eggs, fruit, fish, carrion, and human-adjacent scraps when available.
critters.activeFact: Right now, raccoons may patrol ponds, creeks, feeders, campsites, gardens, and trash zones with tiny burglary hands.

critters.name: Opossum
critters.summary: Warm-night cleanup crew patrolling summer edges for insects, fruit, scraps, carrion, and whatever nobody claimed.
critters.fact: Opossums are omnivorous scavengers that eat insects, carrion, fruit, small animals, eggs, and easy seasonal foods.
critters.activeFact: Right now, opossums may wander sheds, brush, compost spots, trails, road edges, and camp margins after dark.

critters.name: Striped Skunk
critters.summary: Striped grub inspector digging through summer soil like every beetle larva signed a confession.
critters.fact: Striped skunks eat grubs, beetles, grasshoppers, eggs, fruit, small animals, and other easy summer foods.
critters.activeFact: Right now, skunks may nose through lawns, damp soil, trails, and field edges where insects are abundant.

critters.name: Flying Squirrel
critters.summary: Silent leafy glider running midnight treetop routes while the daytime woods misses the whole show.
critters.fact: Flying squirrels feed on seeds, fungi, insects, fruit, buds, and nuts while gliding between trees after dark.
critters.activeFact: Right now, nighttime feeders, nest boxes, cavities, and cameras may catch them moving through full summer canopy.

critters.name: Little Brown Bat
critters.summary: Small mosquito hunter looping over warm July ponds, trails, and openings where bugs are fully in business.
critters.fact: Little brown bats use echolocation to catch flying insects, especially around water and wooded openings.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mild humid nights may bring feeding loops over ponds, clearings, trails, and tree lines.

critters.name: Big Brown Bat
critters.summary: Tough summer dusk hunter cruising lights, buildings, and openings for beetles, moths, and night bugs.
critters.fact: Big brown bats often feed on beetles, moths, and other night-flying insects during warm summer evenings.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they may appear near lights, structures, pond edges, and woodland gaps when insects rise after sunset.

critters.name: Barred Owl
critters.summary: Leafy-woods night caller balancing summer hunting with young owls learning the dark-side basics.
critters.fact: By early July, barred owl young may be branching or exploring while adults continue feeding and guarding them.
critters.activeFact: Right now, calls may roll through thick woods at dusk or dawn, with family activity hidden under full summer leaves.

critters.name: Great Horned Owl
critters.summary: Early-nesting heavyweight now watching older young practice independence with full talon drama.
critters.fact: Great horned owls nest early, so by July young birds may be fledged but still relying on adults for food.
critters.activeFact: Right now, adults may guard territories while young owls practice hunting, flying, and looking like haunted feather furniture.

critters.name: Blue Jay
critters.summary: Blue-feathered security department still loud in the canopy where nests and young birds keep tensions high.
critters.fact: Blue jays defend nests and young aggressively, often mobbing hawks, owls, cats, people, or anything suspicious.
critters.activeFact: Right now, blue jays may scold, chase, and mob around feeders, trees, and nest areas with zero indoor voice.

critters.name: Northern Cardinal
critters.summary: Red summer singer still holding brush territory while pairs feed young and start possible second nesting rounds.
critters.fact: Northern cardinals can nest more than once in a season and continue singing through summer breeding activity.
critters.activeFact: Right now, cardinals may sing at dawn, visit feeders, and move through shrubs with food for young.

critters.name: Black-capped Chickadee
critters.summary: Tiny woodland food runner now wrapping nest season or feeding young through leafy shade.
critters.fact: Black-capped chickadees feed nestlings insects, caterpillars, spiders, and other protein-rich foods.
critters.activeFact: Right now, chickadees may move through branches and cavities, carrying small insects or guiding newly fledged young.

critters.name: Tufted Titmouse
critters.summary: Gray little commentator still working leafy trees with family-season noise and feeder confidence.
critters.fact: Tufted titmice nest in cavities and feed young insects while also using seeds and feeders through the warm season.
critters.activeFact: Right now, titmice may call from shaded branches, visit feeders, and move through woods with young nearby.

critters.name: White-breasted Nuthatch
critters.summary: Upside-down bark walker still patrolling trunks for summer insects and family-season food runs.
critters.fact: White-breasted nuthatches forage along bark for insects, seeds, and hidden food while nesting in tree cavities.
critters.activeFact: Right now, nuthatches may creep headfirst down trunks, call from mature trees, and carry food through leafy cover.

critters.name: Downy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Tiny bark drummer now tapping through bug season while young birds learn the tree-hammer lifestyle.
critters.fact: Downy woodpeckers feed on insects in bark and wood, and young may be fledging or following adults by early July.
critters.activeFact: Right now, downies may tap softly through branches, visit suet, and move with young around wooded edges.

critters.name: Hairy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Bigger timber hammer working mature trees where summer insects are tucked behind bark like snacks in a wall.
critters.fact: Hairy woodpeckers use strong bills to uncover insects beneath bark and may still be feeding young in early summer.
critters.activeFact: Right now, hairy woodpeckers may be heard in older timber, working trunks, calling, or bringing food to young.

critters.name: Pileated Woodpecker
critters.summary: Giant forest carpenter blasting through summer ant country like every dead tree owes him money.
critters.fact: Pileated woodpeckers dig large rectangular holes while searching for carpenter ants, beetle larvae, and other wood insects.
critters.activeFact: Right now, loud calls, deep knocking, and fresh wood chips can reveal where pileateds are working shaded woods.

critters.name: Red-bellied Woodpecker
critters.summary: Noisy woodland hammerhead still calling, feeding young, and treating feeder routes like private property.
critters.fact: Red-bellied woodpeckers feed on insects, nuts, fruit, and seeds, and use cavities for nesting.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they may call, scold, visit feeders, and work tree trunks around family territories.

critters.name: Mourning Dove
critters.summary: Soft-voiced summer nester still proving a few twigs and optimism can somehow count as architecture.
critters.fact: Mourning doves can nest multiple times in warm months and often build simple stick-platform nests.
critters.activeFact: Right now, paired doves may coo, carry twigs, feed young, or sit on nests that look legally unfinished.

critters.name: American Goldfinch
critters.summary: Bright yellow seed specialist now moving toward peak nesting timing as thistle and summer seeds build.
critters.fact: American goldfinches nest later than many songbirds, timing breeding with abundant seed-producing plants.
critters.activeFact: Right now, males are bright, flocks are lively, and nesting behavior is heating up as summer seed sources develop.

critters.name: Dark-eyed Junco
critters.summary: Cool-weather ground bird mostly off the local summer stage while warmer-season feeder traffic takes over.
critters.fact: Dark-eyed juncos usually breed farther north or in cooler highland habitats and are less common in many lower summer areas.
critters.activeFact: Right now, juncos may be scarce around regular feeders as summer birds dominate the ground and brush traffic.

critters.name: House Finch
critters.summary: Social red-headed singer now fully in summer neighborhood mode with nests, feeders, and constant music.
critters.fact: House finches often raise multiple broods and sing frequently around buildings, shrubs, feeders, and sheltered nest sites.
critters.activeFact: Right now, house finches may be singing, paired, feeding young, and making steady feeder visits.

critters.name: Purple Finch
critters.summary: Rosy northern woodland singer still possible around forest edges, arriving like a surprise guest with wings.
critters.fact: Purple finches breed in northern forests and use mixed or coniferous woodland habitat during summer.
critters.activeFact: Right now, purple finches may show at forest edges, feeders, or conifer cover, but visits can stay brief and unpredictable.

critters.name: Wild Turkey
critters.summary: Big ground bird now shifting hard into hen-and-poult season while gobbler drama fades into reruns.
critters.fact: By early July, many wild turkey hens may be leading poults through grassy openings and insect-rich cover.
critters.activeFact: Right now, hens may be secretive with poults, using fields, trails, and brushy edges where young birds can feed on insects.

critters.name: Ruffed Grouse
critters.summary: Forest floor specialist now relying on leafy cover while hens move broods through bug-rich understory.
critters.fact: Ruffed grouse chicks feed heavily on insects early in life and follow hens through dense ground cover.
critters.activeFact: Right now, grouse may be quiet and hidden, with broods tucked into leafy cover where insects are abundant.

critters.name: Sandhill Crane
critters.summary: Tall marsh dinosaur now moving through colt season with loud calls and big wetland family energy.
critters.fact: Sandhill crane colts can walk soon after hatching and follow parents through wetlands and open feeding areas.
critters.activeFact: Right now, cranes may guard young, feed in fields or marshes, and call loudly across open wet ground.

critters.name: American Crow
critters.summary: Black-feathered summer investigator still auditing roads, fields, nests, predators, and anything suspicious.
critters.fact: American crows are intelligent family birds that may defend young, mob predators, and forage widely in summer.
critters.activeFact: Right now, crows may be loud at sunrise, watching hawks, working roadsides, and keeping the neighborhood under review.

critters.name: Common Raven
critters.summary: Big woodland brain cruising summer skies in pairs or family groups like it already knows the ending.
critters.fact: Common ravens often breed early, and by summer young birds may travel with adults through forested territory.
critters.activeFact: Right now, ravens may soar over timber, call from ridges, and move familiar routes in pairs or family groups.

critters.name: Red-tailed Hawk
critters.summary: Soaring field hunter now tied to summer young while careless rodents keep making final decisions.
critters.fact: Red-tailed hawks feed young mammals, birds, reptiles, and other prey to nestlings or fledglings in early summer.
critters.activeFact: Right now, red-tails may perch near fields, circle thermals, and return toward nest areas with prey.

critters.name: Cooper’s Hawk
critters.summary: Fast leafy-tree missile hunting songbirds while summer cover turns every chase into branch-level chaos.
critters.fact: Cooper’s hawks specialize in hunting birds through wooded cover and may still be feeding young in early July.
critters.activeFact: Right now, quick low flights through trees may mean a Cooper’s hawk is hunting or defending family space.

critters.name: Mallard Duck
critters.summary: Classic pond duck now loafing, molting, feeding, and leading ducklings through warm shallow water.
critters.fact: Mallard ducklings feed themselves soon after hatching, while hens guide broods through protective shallow habitat.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mallards may use pond edges, flooded vegetation, ditches, and calm shallows with ducklings close.

critters.name: Wood Duck
critters.summary: Flashy summer backwater duck hiding broods in green pockets where ducklings look like floating punctuation.
critters.fact: Wood duck ducklings leave cavity nests soon after hatching and follow the hen to sheltered water.
critters.activeFact: Right now, wood ducks may stay tucked into shaded pond edges, flooded timber, creek bends, and quiet brood cover.

critters.name: Canada Goose
critters.summary: Pond security staff still escorting goslings and judging every creature within honking distance.
critters.fact: Canada geese guard goslings closely through early summer while feeding on grasses and aquatic plants.
critters.activeFact: Right now, goose families may graze near ponds and fields, with adults watching everything like unpaid bouncers.

critters.name: Great Blue Heron
critters.summary: Long-legged shoreline hunter now stalking fish, frogs, and unlucky shallows with frozen-statue patience.
critters.fact: Great blue herons hunt fish, frogs, crayfish, snakes, and small animals in shallow water through summer.
critters.activeFact: Right now, herons may work pond edges, creek shallows, marsh margins, and quiet shorelines where prey is active.

critters.name: Painted Turtle
critters.summary: Solar-powered pond stacker basking on logs while nesting season keeps some turtles wandering overland.
critters.fact: Painted turtles bask to regulate body temperature, and females may travel to sandy or soft soil to lay eggs.
critters.activeFact: Right now, turtles may stack on logs, cross warm banks, or appear near nesting spots on sunny days.

critters.name: Snapping Turtle
critters.summary: Swamp tank still active in warm water, with nesting wanderers occasionally making the woods feel prehistoric.
critters.fact: Female snapping turtles often travel overland in late spring and early summer to find egg-laying sites.
critters.activeFact: Right now, snapping turtles may move through shallows, cross banks, or appear away from water while nesting activity winds down.

critters.name: Bluegill
critters.summary: Little panfish brawler now deep in warm shallow season where beds, colonies, and attitude all matter.
critters.fact: Bluegill spawn in colonies, with males guarding circular nests in warm shallow water.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bluegill may guard beds, feed near weeds, or gather around firm-bottom shallows and protected edges.

critters.name: Largemouth Bass
critters.summary: Warm-water ambush bruiser shifting from spawning duty into summer cover patrol.
critters.fact: After spawning, largemouth bass often move toward weeds, wood, shade, and structure where prey is available.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bass may hold near logs, weeds, drop-offs, shaded banks, and shallow cover during cooler parts of the day.

critters.name: Yellow Perch
critters.summary: Striped school fish now settled into summer feeding lanes around weeds, edges, and cooler water.
critters.fact: Yellow perch spawn early in spring, then shift into summer feeding patterns around schools, weeds, and structure.
critters.activeFact: Right now, perch may school near weed lines, deeper edges, and food-rich structure as water warms.

critters.name: Northern Pike
critters.summary: Toothy green ambush missile lurking in weedy summer lanes where frogs and fish make terrible choices.
critters.fact: After early spring spawning, northern pike use vegetation, creek mouths, and cooler edge habitat to ambush prey.
critters.activeFact: Right now, pike may hold near weeds, flooded cover, shaded edges, and ambush routes with full villain energy.

critters.name: Spring Peeper
critters.summary: Tiny spring chorus legend now mostly fading back as louder summer frogs take over the night shift.
critters.fact: Spring peepers call most heavily in spring, with activity usually fading as summer warmth and later frogs dominate.
critters.activeFact: Right now, peepers may be quieter, though damp nights or wet woods can still produce scattered calls.

critters.name: American Bullfrog
critters.summary: Big pond bass drum now fully entering summer voice season from warm weedy shorelines.
critters.fact: American bullfrogs call during warm summer breeding periods and hunt insects, fish, frogs, and small animals.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bullfrogs may call from pond edges, sit in warm shallows, and lunge at anything bite-sized.

critters.name: Green Frog
critters.summary: Shoreline banjo frog now active in warm pond edges where bugs, cover, and sound all stack up.
critters.fact: Green frogs call through warm months and often stay close to ponds, wetlands, ditches, and shoreline cover.
critters.activeFact: Right now, green frogs may call from sunny banks, weedy edges, and shallow water with their rubber-band plunk.

critters.name: Leopard Frog
critters.summary: Spotted wet-edge jumper roaming summer grass, puddles, marshes, and damp trail pockets.
critters.fact: Leopard frogs use wet meadows, marsh edges, grass, and shallow water after spring breeding.
critters.activeFact: Right now, leopard frogs may appear near wet grass, puddled trails, marsh edges, and damp cover after rain.

critters.name: Garter Snake
critters.summary: Summer sun noodle fully on patrol for worms, frogs, small fish, and anything slow enough to be lunch.
critters.fact: Garter snakes feed on worms, amphibians, fish, slugs, and small prey during warm-season activity.
critters.activeFact: Right now, garter snakes may bask on trails, hunt near wet spots, or slip through grass where frogs are moving.

critters.name: Water Snake
critters.summary: Bank-side attitude rope now in full shoreline season, hunting fish and frogs from sunny water edges.
critters.fact: Water snakes hunt fish and amphibians around ponds, streams, marshes, and sunny banks during summer.
critters.activeFact: Right now, water snakes may bask near pond edges, dive into shallows, or hold tight to warm banks.

critters.name: Eastern Milk Snake
critters.summary: Secretive warm-cover snake hunting rodents and small prey around woodpiles, barns, rocks, and brush.
critters.fact: Eastern milk snakes eat rodents, small reptiles, eggs, and other prey while using warm cover during summer.
critters.activeFact: Right now, milk snakes may show near boards, barns, wood piles, rocks, and brushy edges on mild days.

critters.name: Monarch Butterfly
critters.summary: Orange milkweed traveler now much more possible as summer nectar and host plants hit stride.
critters.fact: Monarch caterpillars rely on milkweed, while adults use nectar flowers through summer generations.
critters.activeFact: Right now, monarchs may appear around milkweed, meadow flowers, gardens, and sunny openings.

critters.name: Swallowtail Butterfly
critters.summary: Big-winged summer showoff cruising sunny flowers like the meadow finally turned the lights on.
critters.fact: Swallowtails use nectar flowers as adults and lay eggs on specific host plants depending on species.
critters.activeFact: Right now, swallowtails may cruise gardens, trail edges, flowering shrubs, and sunny openings on calm warm days.

critters.name: Dragonfly
critters.summary: Aerial mosquito patrol now fully taking stations around ponds, marshes, trails, and sunny water edges.
critters.fact: Dragonflies spend youth as aquatic nymphs, then emerge as fast-flying adults that hunt insects in the air.
critters.activeFact: Right now, dragonflies may patrol pond edges, hover over trails, and snatch mosquitoes and flies midair.

critters.name: Damselfly
critters.summary: Delicate shoreline flyer now hovering through reeds and pond plants like summer switched on tiny wings.
critters.fact: Damselflies emerge from aquatic nymphs and often stay near vegetation around ponds, streams, and wetlands.
critters.activeFact: Right now, damselflies may perch on reeds, hover near calm water, and flick through shoreline plants on warm days.

critters.name: Firefly
critters.summary: Summer lantern crew now flashing through warm evening grass like the woods installed tiny signal lights.
critters.fact: Adult fireflies use flashing light patterns to find mates during warm summer nights.
critters.activeFact: Right now, fireflies may glow over grass, woodland edges, and damp low spots after sunset.

critters.name: Honeybee
critters.summary: Flower-shift worker now clocking heavy hours across clover, gardens, blossoms, and sunny July edges.
critters.fact: Honeybees collect nectar and pollen from many summer flowers and communicate good food sources with waggle dances.
critters.activeFact: Right now, honeybees may be steady on clover, garden blooms, meadow flowers, and sunny trail edges.

critters.name: Bumblebee
critters.summary: Fuzzy summer pollinator crew now fully active as colonies grow and flowers keep the buffet open.
critters.fact: By early July, bumblebee colonies are growing, with workers foraging for nectar and pollen to support the nest.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bumblebees may work clover, garden flowers, shrubs, meadow blooms, and edges where pollen is reliable.

critters.name: Grasshopper
critters.summary: Summer hopper crew now clicking into gear as grasses thicken and sunny trail edges get lively.
critters.fact: Grasshoppers become more active in summer as warm weather, grasses, and herbaceous plants provide food and cover.
critters.activeFact: Right now, young grasshoppers may jump through sunny grass, dry openings, field edges, and trail margins.
